behaviors.description: The content passed to the summary-generation step includes data retrieved from all three sources
behaviors.justification: The aggregation logic coordinates invocation of three separate data sources and combines their outputs into a single structure. A unit test can mock each source independently and verify: (1) all three sources are invoked, (2) their data is included in the aggregated output, (3) the combined data is passed to the summary generator. The failure mode—one source is never queried due to a code bug—is caught by asserting all three source mocks are called. No coordination between separate processes or external infrastructure is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that mocks the metrics endpoint, issue tracker API, and deployment log sources, calls the data aggregation function, and asserts that all three sources were invoked and their data appears in the aggregated output passed to the summary generator.

behaviors.description: When one data source is unavailable, the report is produced with a `data unavailable` note for that section rather than aborting
behaviors.justification: Error handling for source failures is logic contained within the aggregation function. A unit test can independently mock each source to raise an exception, mock the others to return valid data, and verify the aggregation succeeds with a `data unavailable` marker for the failed source. The failure mode—aggregation crashes instead of gracefully degrading—is testable by asserting the function handles exceptions and returns a partially-complete result. No external dependencies or inter-process coordination required.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with three parametrized cases (one for each source failing). For each case: mock the failing source to raise an exception, mock the other two to return valid data, call the aggregation function, and assert it returns success with the two successful sources' data plus a `data unavailable` marker.

behaviors.description: The narrative summary accurately represents the key values present in the input data
behaviors.justification: If the summary generator is rule-based or template-based (typical for engineering reports), a unit test can verify specific input values appear in the output. However, if the summary is AI-generated (via LLM), accuracy depends on the real model's non-deterministic output, requiring an agentic test. Assuming a deterministic summary implementation (more common), unit testing with fixed input data and assertion on output text is sufficient to catch the failure 'a key value is omitted from the summary.'
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that creates input data with known, specific key values (e.g., deployment count, uptime percentage, issue count), calls the summary generation function, and asserts the output text contains or accurately reflects those values via substring matching or structured field extraction.

behaviors.description: A complete report—from data collection through email delivery—reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly
behaviors.justification: Mocking the email service tests only the code path (that the script invokes send with correct data), not actual delivery. Real-world failures occur outside the mock's scope: SMTP server misconfiguration, network unreachability, DNS resolution failure, authentication failure, or server rate-limiting. To catch failures that prevent actual inbox delivery, the test must use real SMTP infrastructure and a real (test) mailbox. This requires System-level testing with an actual mail server.
behaviors.test_description: System test that runs the full pipeline (data collection via test mocks, summary generation, email delivery) against a real test email server (e.g., MailHog, test SMTP account, or containerized mail service), transmits the email, and verifies it arrives in the test inbox with correct recipient address, subject line, and content.

behaviors.description: When the email delivery service is unreachable, the generated report is saved to a local file and delivery is retried on the next scheduled run
behaviors.justification: This behavior couples two concerns: (1) fallback file I/O and (2) multi-run retry orchestration. File I/O failures (permissions denied, disk full, directory does not exist, encoding issues) cannot be caught by mocks alone—they require a real filesystem. Additionally, the retry logic spans two scheduled invocations with persistent state on disk between them, requiring integration-level coordination. A pure unit test with mocked file I/O would pass even if real disk operations fail; an integration test using a real temp directory and simulating consecutive runs catches the full behavioral contract.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that: (1) runs the report pipeline with email service unavailable (via exception or unreachable SMTP), asserts a fallback file is written to a real temp directory with correct content, (2) simulates a second scheduled run, asserts the script detects the pending report file, (3) retries email delivery (with SMTP mocked as successful this time), and (4) verifies the fallback file is deleted after successful delivery.
